description: The heart of GPU-accelerated machine learning: matrix multiplication from first principles to Tensor Core utilization. Covers GEMM tiling hierarchies, WMMA API, cuBLAS, CUTLASS abstractions, mixed precision, and batched operations.
sources: books/Programming Massively Parallel Processors.pdf (Kirk & Hwu) — Ch3 §3.3 Matrix Multiplication Example, Ch5 §5.3 Tiled Matrix Multiply, Ch6 §6.6 Thread Granularity

concepts.title: GEMM: General Matrix Multiply
concepts.preciseDefinition: C = αA×B + βC where A is M×K, B is K×N, C is M×N. Total FLOPs: 2MNK (one multiply + one accumulate per output element). Arithmetic intensity scales as O(N) for square matrices: 2N³ FLOPs / (4N² bytes × 3 matrices) = N/6 FLOPs/byte. GEMM becomes compute-bound at N > ridge_point × 6 ≈ 1770 for H100 (BF16 Tensor Core).
keyFacts: FLOPs = 2MNK (multiply + accumulate per element); for square MxM: 2M³
H100 ridge point (BF16 TC): 989 TFLOPS / 3.35 TB/s = 295 FLOPs/byte → N ≥ 295×6 ≈ 1770
A100 ridge point (BF16): 312 / 2.0 = 156 FLOPs/byte → N ≥ 936 for compute-bound
cuBLAS achieves ~95% of Tensor Core peak for large square GEMM
Column-major convention: cuBLAS uses Fortran column-major; trick: C = A@B ↔ C^T = B^T @ A^T to avoid transposition
commonMisconceptions: GEMM is always compute-bound — only for large N; small N (e.g., N<256) is still memory-bound
Memory-bound GEMM cannot be improved — increasing batch size or fusing operations improves effective arithmetic intensity
cuBLAS automatically uses Tensor Cores — only for certain data types (FP16/BF16/TF32) and sufficiently large matrices

concepts.title: Three-Level Tiling Hierarchy for GEMM
concepts.preciseDefinition: High-performance GEMM uses a three-level tiling hierarchy: (1) Grid-level tile (block tile): each thread block computes a BM×BN output tile by streaming over K in BK-sized steps; (2) Warp-level tile: each warp computes a WM×WN subtile using register blocking; (3) Thread-level tile: each thread accumulates a TM×TN fragment. This hierarchy maps to GPU memory levels: global→shared (L1) at block level, shared→register at warp level.
keyFacts: Typical H100 block tile: 128×128 with K-tile 32 — loads 128*32*4B = 16KB per matrix into shared memory
Register blocking: 8×8 register tile per thread × 16 threads/warp = 128×8 warp tile
Double buffering: preload next K-tile into alternate shared memory buffer while computing current tile
Shared memory layout: load in column-major for A (to avoid bank conflicts during warp load), row-major for B
CUTLASS implements all three levels as template parameters: threadblock_shape, warp_shape, instruction_shape
commonMisconceptions: Larger tiles always perform better — tile size is limited by shared memory capacity and occupancy
The tiling is the same for all GPU architectures — optimal tile sizes differ significantly between Ampere and Hopper
One should always implement GEMM from scratch — cuBLAS or CUTLASS are the right tools except when custom epilogues are needed

exercises.title: Optimized SGEMM from Scratch
exercises.whatItTeaches: Full optimization journey: naïve → shared memory tiling → register blocking → vectorized loads (float4) → double buffering. Understanding why each step matters by measuring with CUDA events.
exercises.starterCodeHint: // Thread-block: BM=128, BN=128, BK=32; Warp: WM=64, WN=64; Thread: TM=8, TN=8; // Use float4 ldg for coalesced global loads
exercises.testCaseHint: Compare vs cuBLAS at M=N=K=512,1024,2048,4096; target: >70% of cuBLAS throughput

concepts.title: WMMA: Warp-Level Matrix Multiply Accumulate API
concepts.preciseDefinition: The CUDA C++ API for accessing Tensor Cores. A warp collectively loads matrix fragments into registers with wmma::load_matrix_sync(), performs MMA with wmma::mma_sync(), and stores results with wmma::store_matrix_sync(). Fragment layouts are opaque (compiler-defined). Supported shapes (cc7.0+): m16n16k16 (FP16→FP32), m8n32k16, m32n8k16. Fragments are distributed across warp threads.
keyFacts: #include <mma.h>; using namespace nvcuda::wmma;
fragment<matrix_a, M, N, K, half, row_major> a; load_matrix_sync(a, ptr, lda);
mma_sync(c, a, b, c); stores result back into c accumulator
Ampere adds TF32 support: wmma::precision::tf32 for FP32 inputs with TF32 accumulation
Hopper WGMMA: warpgroup MMA across 4 warps; exposed via cutlass::arch::Wgmma or inline PTX
commonMisconceptions: WMMA fragments can be freely read/written per thread — fragment layout is opaque; must use load/store APIs
WMMA is the only way to use Tensor Cores — cuBLAS, CUTLASS, and inline PTX are alternatives
TF32 is the same as FP32 — TF32 has FP32 exponent range but only 10 mantissa bits (vs 23 for FP32)

concepts.title: Mixed Precision Training and Inference
concepts.preciseDefinition: Using lower-precision floating point types (FP16, BF16, FP8, FP4) for compute while accumulating in higher precision (FP32). FP16: 5-bit exponent, 10-bit mantissa — overflow risk. BF16: 8-bit exponent, 7-bit mantissa — same range as FP32, preferred for training. FP8 (E4M3, E5M2): 2× throughput vs FP16; requires loss scaling or quantization. FP4 (Blackwell): 4× vs FP8; for inference.
keyFacts: Ampere A100: FP16 TC = 312 TFLOPS; TF32 TC = 156 TFLOPS; FP32 CUDA = 19.5 TFLOPS
Hopper H100: BF16/FP16 = 989 TFLOPS; FP8 = 1979 TFLOPS
Blackwell B200: BF16 = 2250 TFLOPS; FP8 = 4500 TFLOPS; FP4 = 9000 TFLOPS
BF16 preferred over FP16 for training: same exponent range as FP32 avoids overflow without loss scaling
FP8 requires calibration or dynamic scaling to handle range differences (E4M3 vs E5M2 tradeoffs)
commonMisconceptions: FP16 and BF16 are interchangeable — BF16 has larger range, FP16 has more precision; training often needs BF16
Mixed precision always doubles throughput — only when bottleneck is Tensor Core arithmetic, not memory bandwidth
FP8 training requires no changes to the training loop — FP8 requires careful scaling to avoid underflow

exercises.title: HGEMM with WMMA Tensor Core API
exercises.whatItTeaches: Using wmma::load_matrix_sync, wmma::mma_sync, wmma::store_matrix_sync; tiling GEMM at the warp level for WMMA fragments; understanding that fragment layout is opaque; comparing to cuBLAS cublasGemmEx.
exercises.starterCodeHint: fragment<matrix_a,16,16,16,half,row_major> a; load_matrix_sync(a, A_tile_ptr, K); fragment<accumulator,16,16,16,float> c; fill_fragment(c,0.f); mma_sync(c,a,b,c); store_matrix_sync(C_ptr, c, N, mem_row_major);
exercises.testCaseHint: Verify result matches cublasGemmEx with CUBLAS_COMPUTE_32F; benchmark at M=N=K=1024/4096

concepts.title: cuBLAS API
concepts.preciseDefinition: NVIDIA's closed-source implementation of BLAS on GPU. Key function: cublasGemmEx(handle, trA, trB, M, N, K, alpha, A, CUDA_R_16F, lda, B, CUDA_R_16F, ldb, beta, C, CUDA_R_16F, ldc, CUBLAS_COMPUTE_32F, CUBLAS_GEMM_DEFAULT_TENSOR_OP). Requires column-major layout (Fortran convention); row-major trick: compute C^T = B^T @ A^T by swapping A/B and their dimensions.
keyFacts: cublasHandle_t: opaque context; create with cublasCreate(), destroy with cublasDestroy()
Column-major by default; CUBLAS_OP_T transposes matrix in-place for computation
cublasSetMathMode(handle, CUBLAS_TENSOR_OP_MATH) enables Tensor Core usage
Batched GEMM: cublasGemmBatchedEx or cublasGemmStridedBatchedEx for arrays of matrices
Workspace: cublasSetWorkspace for tuned algorithms requiring scratch memory
commonMisconceptions: cuBLAS always uses Tensor Cores — only with CUBLAS_COMPUTE_16F or CUBLAS_TENSOR_OP_MATH mode
cuBLAS column-major means slower — row-major matrices can be used with the A↔B swap trick at zero cost
cuBLAS is always the fastest GEMM — for small matrices or custom epilogues, CUTLASS or custom kernels can be faster

concepts.title: Batched GEMM
concepts.preciseDefinition: Computing multiple independent GEMMs simultaneously in a single cuBLAS call. Two variants: (1) cublasGemmBatchedEx: array of pointers, matrices can be non-contiguous; (2) cublasGemmStridedBatchedEx: strided layout, all matrices at fixed stride. Important for attention heads (B × head_dim × seq_len) and batch processing where each sample has an independent GEMM.
keyFacts: Strided batched: O(batch_size × M × N × K) with stride_A, stride_B, stride_C
Pointer batched: allows non-uniform matrix sizes across batch
Multi-head attention: compute Q@K^T as batched GEMM (batch=num_heads, M=seq_len, N=seq_len, K=head_dim)
cuBLAS picks optimized kernel based on batch_size, M, N, K at runtime
Small batch (<= 16) with small matrices: often better to fuse into a single custom kernel
commonMisconceptions: Batched GEMM is just looping over cublasGemm — a single batched call can be significantly more efficient
Strided batched requires contiguous memory across batch — strides allow arbitrary layout within the batch

exercises.title: cuBLAS GEMM Integration
exercises.whatItTeaches: cublasHandle lifecycle; cublasGemmEx with FP16 inputs and FP32 accumulation; row-major ↔ column-major transpose trick; measuring vs custom kernel.
exercises.starterCodeHint: cublasGemmEx(handle, CUBLAS_OP_N, CUBLAS_OP_N, N, M, K, &alpha, B, CUDA_R_16F, N, A, CUDA_R_16F, K, &beta, C, CUDA_R_16F, N, CUBLAS_COMPUTE_32F, CUBLAS_GEMM_DEFAULT_TENSOR_OP);
exercises.testCaseHint: Verify against CPU reference; benchmark vs custom SGEMM at M=N=K=2048

concepts.title: CUTLASS: CUDA Templates for Linear Algebra Subroutines
concepts.preciseDefinition: An open-source NVIDIA library of C++ template abstractions for high-performance GEMM at all levels of the GPU memory hierarchy. Decomposes GEMM into: (1) GemmKernel — threadblock-level scheduling, (2) Mma — warp-level MMA pipeline, (3) Epilogue — post-GEMM operations. Version 4 adds CuTe DSL: Python-native, architecture-generic kernel programming for Hopper and Blackwell.
keyFacts: CUTLASS 4.4.1 (Feb 2026): supports Volta→Blackwell; FP64/FP32/TF32/FP16/BF16/FP8/INT8/INT4/FP4/MXFP6/MXFP8
CuTe (Cooperating Threads) abstraction: Tensor = Layout + Pointer; Layout = (shape, stride) pair
Hopper: cutlass::gemm::collective::CollectiveMma with wgmma::MmaOperand for WGMMA
Epilogue Visitor Tree (EVT): compose post-GEMM operations (bias, activation, quantization) as expression trees
Block-scaled types: NVFP4, MXFP4, MXFP6, MXFP8 for Blackwell FP4 block-scaled GEMM
commonMisconceptions: CUTLASS is just a wrapper around cuBLAS — CUTLASS is source-level; enables custom epilogues, mixed-precision, and new architectures
CUTLASS is too complex to use directly — CuTe DSL in CUTLASS 4 provides Python-native interface
CUTLASS only works with specific matrix sizes — CUTLASS handles arbitrary sizes via tilizing and padding

coverageGaps: Sparse GEMM (cuSPARSE, structured sparsity) — Ampere 2:4 sparsity doubles Tensor Core throughput; no topic planned
Conv2D as GEMM (im2col) — important for deep learning; no exercise planned
CUTLASS hands-on exercise — theory covered but no practice exercise due to complexity
